Anthropic’s decision to limit the release of its powerful new AI model, Mythos, has sparked debate over whether the move is primarily about protecting global cybersecurity or safeguarding the company’s strategic interests. The model has demonstrated an extraordinary ability to identify and exploit software vulnerabilities across widely used systems, raising concerns that public access could enable large-scale cyberattacks.
 

 To mitigate these risks, Anthropic has opted for a controlled rollout under its “Project Glasswing” initiative, granting access only to a select group of trusted organizations focused on defensive cybersecurity. This approach allows vulnerabilities to be discovered and patched before malicious actors can exploit them. However, critics question whether restricting access also consolidates power among a small group of major tech players, potentially shaping the competitive landscape of AI.
 

 The situation highlights a broader dilemma in AI development: as models become increasingly capable, companies must balance openness with safety. Anthropic’s cautious strategy may set a precedent for future AI releases, where limiting access becomes a necessary step to manage real-world risks while navigating competitive pressures.

The U.S. Department of Justice has launched an antitrust investigation into the National Football League, focusing on whether its television and streaming deals unfairly limit competition and increase costs for consumers. The probe is examining the league’s media contracts, which increasingly require fans to subscribe to multiple platforms to watch games.
 

 At the center of the investigation is the NFL’s long-standing ability to collectively negotiate broadcast rights under the Sports Broadcasting Act of 1961. While this exemption applies to traditional broadcast TV, regulators are questioning whether it still holds in an era dominated by cable and streaming services.
 

 Critics argue that spreading games across platforms like Amazon Prime Video, Netflix, and ESPN has made it more expensive and complicated for fans to follow the sport, sometimes requiring multiple subscriptions. Meanwhile, the NFL maintains that most games remain available on free local broadcasts and that its model benefits both fans and media partners.
 

 The investigation could have significant implications for how sports media rights are structured, potentially reshaping the balance between accessibility, competition, and the rapidly evolving streaming ecosystem.
ai_title: U.S. Justice Department Probes NFL Media Deals Over Antitrust Concerns

title: How bad for humans is wildlife trade? A new study has answers - NPR
description: People sell wild animals for food and for traditional medicine — legally and illegally. A study looks at the risks of spillover diseases from those pangolins, giant rats and other exotic critters.
content:
In 2003, a shipment of exotic African rodents to a pet store in Illinois sparked the United States' first mpox outbreak
 . Gambian giant rats and other rodents infected prairie dogs, which in turn in… [+5632 chars]
ai_summary:
A growing body of research highlights how the global wildlife trade is significantly increasing the risk of dangerous pathogens spilling over from animals to humans, raising concerns about future pandemics. Scientists say that the trade—spanning exotic pets, food markets, and animal products—creates frequent and intense contact between humans and wildlife, providing ideal conditions for viruses and bacteria to jump species.
 

 A recent study analyzing decades of data found that animals involved in wildlife trade are far more likely to carry diseases that can infect humans compared to those not traded. The longer a species circulates in trade networks, the greater the number of pathogens it can transmit, compounding the risk over time.
 

 Experts emphasize that the highest-risk stages occur early in the supply chain—during hunting, transport, and handling—when animals are stressed and often kept in unsanitary conditions. These environments allow pathogens to spread rapidly across species and potentially reach humans.
 

 The findings reinforce concerns that wildlife trade has contributed to past outbreaks such as Ebola, HIV, and COVID-19, and underscore the urgent need for stronger global regulation, surveillance, and prevention strategies to reduce the likelihood of future health crises.
ai_title: Global Wildlife Trade Is Accelerating Disease Spillover Risks, Scientists Warn

The second incident took place on April 7 near Holiday Park Road in Hillsborough, where an outdoor cat bit a person who is currently receiving rabies prophylaxis treatment.
 Officials from the Orange County Health Department are evaluating exposure risks on a case-by-case basis. For vaccinated pets potentially exposed to rabies, a booster shot must be administered within 96 hours. Unvaccinated pets face either euthanasia or quarantine for up to four months.
 Authorities are reminding residents that all mammals can carry and transmit rabies, with raccoons, skunks, foxes, and bats being the most common carriers in North Carolina. title: Artemis II astronauts have fitness lessons for everyone - NPR
description: The Artemis II astronauts don't have a lot of space to exercise. That's why they've got the flywheel — a small device that can be used for strength and cardio workouts.
content: Even a few days away from Earth can significantly alter the human body. Without the constant pull of gravity on the skeleton, muscle and bone can quickly atrophy. To combat this immediate physical de… [+4377 chars]
ai_summary:
The four Artemis II astronauts aboard NASA's Orion spacecraft are using a compact exercise device called the flywheel to combat the rapid muscle and bone loss that occurs in microgravity. Even a few days without gravity can cause significant physical decline, particularly in the heart and skeletal muscles, making regular exercise critical during the mission.
 The flywheel, roughly the size of a large shoebox, works on a cable-and-resistance principle similar to a yo-yo — astronauts pull a handle to spin the device, generating resistance proportional to their effort. Despite its small size, it can simulate resistance equivalent to up to 400 pounds, allowing for exercises like squats, deadlifts, and curls, as well as cardiovascular workouts.
 The device was developed after years of research, including a NASA bed-rest study where over 10,000 volunteers applied to spend 70 days lying down — earning the nickname "pillownauts." Researchers found the flywheel prevented muscle atrophy as effectively as a full gym setup.
 Scientists believe the technology has broad applications beyond space travel, potentially helping elderly individuals combat age-related bone and muscle loss, and someday fitting neatly under a desk for everyday use.
ai_title: How the Artemis II Crew's Flywheel Device Is Redefining Space Fitness for Everyone

title: Scientists say the world’s oldest octopus fossil isn’t an octopus after all - AP News
description: Scientists have found evidence that a 300-million-year-old sea creature previously thought to be the world's oldest octopus is actually a nautilus relative. University of Reading zoology lecturer Thomas Clements led the research, which found that the fossil, …
content:
LONDON (AP) A 300-million-year-old tentacled sea creature has lost its crown as the worlds oldest octopus, after scientists found evidence that its not an octopus at all.
 Newly published research co… [+3388 chars]
ai_summary:
A fossil long celebrated as the world's oldest known octopus has been stripped of its title after scientists determined it is not an octopus at all. The specimen, known as Pohlsepia mazonensis and found at the Mazon Creek site in Illinois, had been listed in Guinness World Records as the earliest known octopus since its identification in 2000. New research published in the journal Proceedings of the Royal Society B concludes it is actually a relative of the modern nautilus.
 The breakthrough came when University of Reading zoologist Thomas Clements used synchrotron imaging — which generates X-rays using beams of light brighter than the sun — to scan inside the fossil rock. The technique revealed a radula, a ribbon-like feeding structure with at least 11 teeth per row. Octopuses have only seven or nine teeth per row, while nautiloids have 13, definitively ruling out an octopus classification.
 The misidentification is believed to have occurred because the creature decomposed and lost its distinctive shell before fossilisation, making it appear octopus-like. Guinness World Records has since removed the fossil's title. The finding pushes back the true origin of octopuses to the Jurassic period, and the Mazon Creek specimens now represent the oldest known nautiloid soft tissue in the fossil record.
ai_title: 300-Million-Year-Old "World's Oldest Octopus" Fossil Reclassified as Nautilus

Barcelona have lodged an official complaint with UEFA following their 2-0 Champions League quarterfinal first leg defeat to Atletico Madrid at Camp Nou on April 8, 2026. The club's legal department filed the complaint the following day, focusing on a controversial incident in the 54th minute involving Atletico substitute Marc Pubill.
 The incident occurred when Atletico goalkeeper Juan Musso played a short goal-kick, bringing the ball into play. Pubill then handled the ball inside the penalty area, apparently unaware play had restarted. Referee Istvan Kovacs did not award a penalty and, crucially, VAR official Christian Dingert did not intervene — despite Pubill already being on a yellow card, meaning a second booking and sending-off should also have followed.
 Manager Hansi Flick was furious post-match, calling the non-decision unbelievable. Barcelona referenced a near-identical incident from a 2024 Champions League match between Club Brugge and Aston Villa, where a penalty was correctly awarded in the same circumstances.
 The club has requested a formal investigation, access to referee communications, and official acknowledgment of the error. While the result stands, Barcelona face a difficult second leg in Madrid needing to overturn a two-goal deficit.
ai_title: Barcelona File Formal UEFA Complaint Over VAR Failure in 2-0 Champions League Loss to Atletico

This discontent stems from the league's increasing reliance on subscription-based streaming services and pay-TV channels, making it challenging and expensive for many to watch games without multiple subscriptions. The probe will examine if the NFL's current deals for distributing game broadcasts violate antitrust laws by limiting competition and potentially harming consumers. Critics argue that the fragmented broadcast landscape, with games spread across platforms like Peacock, Amazon Prime Video, and ESPN+, forces fans into numerous costly subscriptions, diminishing the sport's accessibility. This inquiry signals a serious government step to scrutinize how major sports leagues manage media rights and whether these arrangements prioritize profit over fan experience and fair market practices, potentially leading to major changes in future NFL game distribution.

ai_summary: A jury awarded Jennifer Anderson, a Darien woman with terminal cervical cancer, $49 million in a malpractice lawsuit against Westmed Medical Group. Anderson sued her doctor, Dr. Elizabeth McDonald, for failing to follow up on high-risk HPV test results from 2020. This negligence prevented early detection and treatment, leading to her cancer being diagnosed at an advanced, incurable stage. The lawsuit highlighted that standard procedures, such as a colposcopy, could have identified the cancer when it was treatable. The verdict, delivered in Stamford's state Superior Court, covers medical expenses, lost earning capacity, and immense pain and suffering. Attorneys Sarah Russell and Peter Dreyer represented Anderson, arguing that the doctor's failure to adhere to medical protocols directly resulted in her devastating prognosis. This significant ruling provides justice and financial security for Anderson's future care, underscoring the severe consequences of medical negligence in crucial preventative healthcare.
ai_title: Jury awards $49M to Darien woman whose doctor missed likely fatal cancer

This vital component must withstand extreme temperatures and atmospheric friction as the spacecraft plunges at speeds reaching a staggering 24,000 mph. Their very lives are staked on the flawless performance of this engineering marvel.

title: Scientists say the world's oldest octopus fossil isn't an octopus after all - ABC News
description: Scientists have found evidence that a 300-million-year-old sea creature previously thought to be the world's oldest octopus is actually a nautilus relative
content:
LONDON -- A 300-million-year-old tentacled sea creature has lost its crown as the worlds oldest octopus, after scientists found evidence that its not an octopus at all.
 Newly published research conc… [+2919 chars]
ai_summary: New scientific research has overturned a previous understanding about the world's oldest known octopus fossil, a 300-million-year-old creature. Scientists have presented evidence suggesting that the ancient sea animal, once believed to be the earliest octopus, is actually a relative of the nautilus or an ancestor of vampyropods, which include both octopuses and squids. The re-evaluation stems from new observations of the fossil, particularly the number of its appendages. While octopuses are characterized by having eight arms, this ancient creature clearly possessed ten. This discovery significantly pushes back the evolutionary timeline for when octopuses developed their distinctive eight-arm characteristic and reconfigures our understanding of early cephalopod diversification. The finding reshapes the evolutionary tree of these marine invertebrates, implying that octopuses likely emerged later than the age of this particular fossil. This crucial reclassification means the search for the true oldest octopus continues.
ai_title: Scientists say the world's oldest octopus fossil isn't an octopus after all
